coredata.dc:title: Accelerating mechanism of carbon dots-modified biocarrier on anammox process using metagenomic and metatranscriptomic techniques
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Fanghao
Lu, Hong
Li, Qiansheng
Li, Xiaoman
Wang, Jichao
Li, Yifan
Li, Chaoyu
Zhou, Jiti
coredata.dc:description: The rates of anammox and endogenous dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) under autotrophic conditions are usually low due to the slow metabolism rates of anammox bacteria. Carbon dots (CDs) as novel electron mediators can accelerate extracellular and intracellular electron transfer of bacteria. Herein, for the first time, the effect of CDs-modified biocarrier on anammox and endogenous DNRA processes in the absence of additional organic carbon sources was investigated. Results showed that the immobilized CDs (iCDs) could significantly promote the removal rates of NH4 +-N (4.58%∼57.32%), NO2 −-N (7.17%∼42.90%) and TN (7.53%∼58.48%) of the anammox reactor in the nitrogen loading rate range of 1.044∼1.74 kgN/m3/d. During this process, the iCDs could be slowly released and partly enter cells, resulting in the enhanced tightly bound extracellular polymeric substances conductivity and specific anammox activity of biofilm, especially, key enzymes activities of anammox process. Further metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing analysis showed that iCDs addition markedly increased the relative abundances of hzsA/B/C, hao, hdh, nxrA genes related to anammox and narH and nrfA genes related to DNRA in Ca. Brocadia, their expressions in Ca. Brocadia were also remarkably upregulated. Meanwhile, the addition of iCDs increased the relative abundances and expressions of genes associated with signaling molecules, accelerating biofilm formation and associating AnAOB and their mutualism bacteria (Desulfobacillus and UBA12294) more tightly. These findings revealed that iCDs accelerated nitrogen removal via mainly increasing anammox and DNRA rates of Ca. Brocadia, which provides a new strategy to enhance the nitrogen removal of anammox process under autotrophic conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Anammox
Metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing
Endogenous DNRA
Signaling molecules

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nitrogen removal performance of UASB with CDs-modified biocarrier
Characterization of biofilm attached on CDs-modified biocarrier
Electron transport system and activities of key enzymes
Analysis of microbial community in biofilm
Microbial community analysis at the phylum and genus levels
Effects of iCDs on electron production and transport processes
Effect of iCDs on nitrogen metabolism
Effect of iCDs on carbon metabolism
The effect of iCDs on signaling molecules

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176027 ). The authors thank Dr. Xiaona Li for the technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of UASB reactors amended with biocarriers used in this study.

Nitrogen removal performances of two reactors: Effluent NH4 +-N concentration, ANRE, Effluent NO2 −-N concentration, NNRE, Effluent NO3 −-N concentration, Anammox process stoichiometry (ΔNO2 −-N/ΔNH4 +-N and ΔNO3 −-N/ΔNH4 +-N), NLR, NRR, TNRE of (a) R1 and (b) R2.

(a) The SAA of two reactors. (b) The EPS content and components of two reactors. The 3D-EEM spectroscopic of different EPS: LB-EPS of (c) R1 and (d) R2 and TB-EPS of (e) R1 and (f) R2. (g) The EIS of TB-EPS in two reactors. (h) Effects of iCDs on the activities of ETSA, Complex Ⅰ, Complex Ⅲ and Cytc. (i) Effects of iCDs on the activities of key enzymes in anammox process. (ns ≥ 0.05, * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001, **** p ≤ 0.0001).

Effect of iCDs on the structure of bacterial community at the (a) phylum level and (b) genus level. Effect of iCDs on the abundances and expressions of various genes coding ATP, Complex Ⅰ, Complex Ⅲ and Cytc (c and d).

Effect of iCDs on the abundances of genes encoding nitrogen cycle (a), the genes variations in the main nitrogen conversion pathways (b) and the contribution analysis in nitrogen metabolism genes (c). Effect of iCDs on the expressions of genes encoding nitrogen cycle (d), the genes expressions in the main nitrogen conversion pathways (e) and on the expressions of genes encoding nitrogen cycle at the genus level (f).

Effect of iCDs on the abundances of genes encoding Wood-Ljungdahl pathway, TCA cycle and glycolysis pathway (a), the genes variations in the main carbon metabolism pathways (b) and the expressions of genes encoding carbon metabolism (c). Effect of iCDs on the abundances of genes encoding signaling molecules (d), the contribution analysis in genes related to signaling molecules (e) and the expressions of genes encoding signaling molecules (f). Possible mechanism of iCDs promoting anammox (g).
The parameters of the long-term operation.

head.ce:title: Accelerating mechanism of carbon dots-modified biocarrier on anammox process using metagenomic and metatranscriptomic techniques

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering
School of Environmental Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rates of anammox and endogenous dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) under autotrophic conditions are usually low due to the slow metabolism rates of anammox bacteria. Carbon dots (CDs) as novel electron mediators can accelerate extracellular and intracellular electron transfer of bacteria. Herein, for the first time, the effect of CDs-modified biocarrier on anammox and endogenous DNRA processes in the absence of additional organic carbon sources was investigated. Results showed that the immobilized CDs (iCDs) could significantly promote the removal rates of NH4 +-N (4.58%∼57.32%), NO2 −-N (7.17%∼42.90%) and TN (7.53%∼58.48%) of the anammox reactor in the nitrogen loading rate range of 1.044∼1.74 kgN/m3/d. During this process, the iCDs could be slowly released and partly enter cells, resulting in the enhanced tightly bound extracellular polymeric substances conductivity and specific anammox activity of biofilm, especially, key enzymes activities of anammox process. Further metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing analysis showed that iCDs addition markedly increased the relative abundances of hzsA/B/C, hao, hdh, nxrA genes related to anammox and narH and nrfA genes related to DNRA in Ca. Brocadia, their expressions in Ca. Brocadia were also remarkably upregulated. Meanwhile, the addition of iCDs increased the relative abundances and expressions of genes associated with signaling molecules, accelerating biofilm formation and associating AnAOB and their mutualism bacteria (Desulfobacillus and UBA12294) more tightly. These findings revealed that iCDs accelerated nitrogen removal via mainly increasing anammox and DNRA rates of Ca. Brocadia, which provides a new strategy to enhance the nitrogen removal of anammox process under autotrophic conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anaerobic ammonia oxidation (anammox), a promising biological nitrogen removal technology, possesses the advantages of energy saving and low consumption [1,2]. However, anammox bacteria (AnAOB) are usually faced with the disadvantages of long doubling time, environmental sensitivity and slow reaction speed in practical application [3]. Moreover, the formation of their metabolic byproduct nitrate inevitably caused secondary pollution to the effluent and reduced the total nitrogen removal in anammox process [4]. Recent studies showed that some anammox bacteria (such as Ca. Brocadia and Ca. Kuenenia, etc) could utilize internal glycogen to endogenous dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA), facilitating the transformation of the byproduct NO3 −-N to NH4 +-N [5,6]. This behavior indicates that the coupling endogenous DNRA with anammox will be beneficial for total nitrogen removal. Therefore, enhancing the above two nitrogen transformation processes of AnAOB is of great significance.

Many studies have shown that some carbon-based materials (such as biochar, graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide, etc.) can improve the nitrogen removal performance by promoting the proliferation of AnAOB, sludge porosity and mass transfer, or by facilitating extracellular electron transfer and enzyme activities [7–9]. As the enzymes related with anammox process are located in cells, the extracellular effects of these carbon-based materials are limited. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) with high conductivity and the sizes of less than 10 nm [10,11], have been found to not only promote interspecies electron transfer under anaerobic digestion [12], but also enter the interior of bacteria. Moreover, intracellular CDs as electron mediators could significantly increase the intracellular charge, metabolic rate and DNRA process of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 [13,14]. It has also been proposed that Se doped-CDs contribute to maintain the redox homeostasis of AnAOB and improve antioxidant enzyme activity and sludge activity [15]. However, little information is available on the effect of CDs on anammox and endogenous DNRA processes of AnAOB in the absence of exogenous organic carbon sources.

Considering the water solubility of CDs, CDs-modified biocarrier was prepared via amide and ester bonds so that the immobilized CDs (iCDs) could be slowly released. In the present study, the nitrogen removal performance of reactor amended with the CDs-modified biocarrier was investigated, during which anammox and endogenous DNRA processes of AnAOB were analyzed. Moreover, the characteristics of the biofilm on the CDs-modified biocarrier were compared with those on the unmodified biocarrier. Using metagenomic and metatranscriptomic approaches, the changes of bacterial communities in the biofilms and their functional genes were also revealed. These studies will provide an insight into the accelerating effect and mechanism of CDs on the removal of nitrogen during anammox process.

CDs were immobilized on nonwoven fabrics according to the previous method [16]. Briefly, the nonwoven fabrics (5 cm × 5 cm) were carboxylatively modified under ultraviolet irradiation, and then 120 mg CDs were dissolved in 15 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide and reacted with carboxylated fabrics in an oil bath at 80°C for 8 h. The prepared CDs-modified fabrics were then fixed on plastic discs, which were strung together with a plastic string. Its two ends were fixed to keep the composite biocarrier suspending in an up-flow anaerobic sludge blanket (UASB) reactor. As shown in Fig. S1, the CDs-modified composite biocarrier contained 11 plastic discs with a total of 66 pieces of CDs-modified nonwoven fabrics.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 , two UASB reactors R1 and R2 (2.0 L) were made from polymethyl methacrylate. Their height and the diameter were 260 mm and 100 mm, respectively. R1 with the unmodified biocarrier and R2 with CDs-modified biocarrier were used as the control and experimental reactors, respectively. Seeding sludge was derived from granular sludge in a long-operating reactor in the laboratory. Each reactor was inoculated with 104.11 g wet sludge and its volatile suspended solids (VSS) concentration was 2 g/L.

The simulated influent wastewater consisted of NH4 +-N and NO2 −-N (supplied by NH4Cl and NaNO2), 0.5 g/L NaHCO3, 0.48 g/L KH2PO4, 0.02 g/L MgSO4, 0.02 g/L CaCl2 and 1.0 mL of a trace element solution per 1 L of wastewater, which was composed of Na2EDTA (5 g/L), FeSO4·7H2O (5 g/L), CuSO4·5H2O (0.5 g/L), ZnSO4·7H2O (0.5 g/L) and MnCl2·4H2O (0.25 g/L). Both reactors were operated in the same continuous flow mode with initial hydraulic retention time (HRT) of 8.0 h. Their reflux ratio was set at 4:1 to improve the mass transfer capacity. The temperature in both reactors was controlled at around 35°C using a thermostatic bath. As shown in Table 1 , the whole operation process of reactors was divided into three phases based on different nitrogen loading rate (NLR). Samples of the influent and effluent were taken every day, filtrated through a 0.22 µm membrane for further analysis.

After 102 days of operation, biofilm samples were taken from two biocarriers in both reactors and washed twice with 27.5 mM PBS buffer (pH=7.4). Then, biofilm samples (0.5 g) were added into 110 mL serum bottles containing 100 mL minimal medium. The minimal medium consisted of NH4Cl (2.5 mM) and NaNO2 (3.3 mM) and the concentrations of other components were the same with those of the simulated initial influent. These bottles were purged with N2 for 20 min and incubated in an incubator at 35°C with 150 rpm. After 19 h of the reaction, samples were filtrated through a 0.22 µm membrane, and then the concentrations of NH4 +-N and NO2 −-N in the supernatants were determined. The specific anammox activity (SAA) of biofilms was calculated based on the consumption of the NH4Cl and NaNO2, and the sludge concentration (VSS) [17].

After 102 days of operation, 0.5 g biofilm samples were taken from two kinds of biocarriers in both reactors. Then, the biofilm samples were washed once with 27.5 mM PBS buffer and harvested using centrifugation (10,000 g, 10 min, 4°C). Subsequently, the extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) of the biofilm samples were extracted according to the heating method [18]. The contents of the polysaccharides (PS) and proteins (PN) in EPS were analyzed by phenol-concentrated sulfuric acid and modified Lowry methods, respectively [19]. The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) spectrograms of the extracted loosely bound layer (LB-EPS) and tightly bound layer (TB-EPS) were measured using a temperature-controlled fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Co., USA). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the TB-EPS was determined according to the method [20].

Biofilm samples from both reactors were collected and refrigerated under liquid nitrogen for subsequent DNA extraction and sequencing. The obtained sequencing data of metagenomics and metatranscriptomics were deposited in the NCBI under BioProject PRJNA1221977 and PRJNA1222490, respectively. Detailed information on the metagenomic and metatranscriptomic were depicted in the Supplementary Materials (Text S1 and Text S2).

The concentrations of VSS, NH4 +-N, NO2 −-N and NO3 −-N were measured according to the methods [21,22]. The concentration of free CDs in the effluent was determined at 363 nm by a UV–VIS spectrophotometry. The activities of cytochrome C (Cytc), nitrite reductase (NIR), hydrazine synthase (HZS), hydrazine dehydrogenase (HDH) and nitrite oxidoreductase (NXR) were determined using ELISA assay kits (Jiangsu Meimian Industrial Co., Ltd, China). The fluorescence of cell extracts was measured using a temperature-controlled fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Co., USA). The internal morphology of AnAOB and the particle size of CDs in AnAOB were analyzed by using biological transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT7800, Japan). The methods of electron transport system activity (ETSA) and other enzyme activities assays could be obtained in the Supplementary Materials (Text S3 and Text S4). The batch test methods used for evaluating the impact of iCDs on nitrate metabolism of AnAOB were available in the Supplementary Materials (Text S5).
All data were obtained in triplicate and presented as mean ± standard deviation. GraphPad Prism 8 was used to analyze whether the differences were significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen removal performance of UASB with CDs-modified biocarrier
ce:section.ce:para: R2 amended with CDs-modified biocarrier was operated and compared with R1 amended with unmodified biocarrier for 102 days along with the gradual increase of NLR from 1.044 kgN/m3/d to 1.74 kgN/m3/d. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, b, in Phase Ⅰ (NLR=1.044 kgN/m3/d, days 1–14), it was observed that the average ammonium nitrogen removal efficiency (ANRE), nitrite nitrogen removal efficiency (NNRE) and total nitrogen removal efficiency (TNRE) of both reactors were increased continually. Meanwhile, the effluent nitrate accumulation also consistently raised. Moreover, the average ANRE, NNRE, and TNRE of R2 were increased by 4.58%, 7.17% and 7.53%, respectively, compared with those of R1. When NLR was elevated to 1.392 kgN/m3/d (Phase Ⅱ, days 15–44), the average ANRE, NNRE, and TNRE of R2 were increased by 11.91%, 11.58% and 14.92%, respectively, compared with those of R1. These results suggested that the addition of CDs-modified biocarrier could promote anammox under low NLR conditions.

When NLR was further increased from 1.392 kgN/m3/d to 1.74 kgN/m3/d (250 mg/L NH4 +-N and 330 mg/L NO2 −-N), the TNRE of R1 began to drop dramatically from 68.82% to 43.19%. In comparison, TNRE of R2 had decreased only from 84.41% to 73.59% and its nitrogen removal performance rapidly recovered within a shorter period of time. These results indicated that R2 had better tolerance to NO2 −-N concentration shock due to the presence of iCDs. In Phase Ⅲ (NLR=1.74 kgN/m3/d, days 45–102), it was surprising that the average ANRE, NNRE, and TNRE of the R2 were increased by 57.32%, 42.90% and 58.48%, respectively, compared with those of R1. This result implied that iCDs significantly enhanced anammox process under high NLR conditions. Further analysis found that the average ratios of ΔNO2 −-N/ΔNH4 +-N and ΔNO3 −-N/ΔNH4 +-N in R2 were 1.29 and 0.27, respectively, which were lower than those in R1 (1.36 and 0.38). It was hypothesized that certain nitrate removal pathways in R2 were enhanced. Hence, the influence of iCDs on the conversion of nitrate was further examined. The seeding sludge was incubated with nitrate as a sole nitrogen source. After 3 h of incubation, NO3 −-N reduction was observed in the control and iCDs-amended groups. Moreover, iCDs addition resulted in the increases of NO3 −-N removal and NH4 +-N production (Fig. S1a–c). These results showed that the endogenous DNRA of AnAOB was enhanced after the addition of iCDs. Meanwhile, the formed NH4 +-N could be removed via the enhanced anammox process, which was beneficial for total nitrogen removal.

Further analysis showed that the average concentration of the CDs in the effluent was 7 mg/L in R2 (Fig. S2a) and the unique fluorescence of CDs at 365 nm of UV light was also observed in R2 (Fig. S2b and c), which demonstrated that iCDs could be slowly released. Moreover, fluorescence analysis of the cell extracts showed that the biofilm samples of R2 had a higher fluorescence intensity peak at the Ex/Em of 350 nm/467 nm, compared with those of R1 (Fig. S3a and b). It was also clearly observed that a large number of free CDs were located in the AnAOB cytoplasm using TEM and their average particle size was 4.88 nm (Fig. S3c–e). These results indicated that the released CDs could enter cells. Previous studies showed that intracellular CDs could enhance cell metabolism and the activities of enzymes [14]. In the present study, our analysis showed that there was 99.61% improvement in the SAA of the biofilm in R2, compared with that in R1 (Fig. 3 a), thereby enhancing nitrogen removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of biofilm attached on CDs-modified biocarrier

ce:section.ce:para: EPS is commonly divided into LB-EPS and TB-EPS [23]. TB-EPS has been reported to benefit the AnAOB aggregation, whereas excess LB-EPS deteriorated aggregate structure and inhibited AnAOB biofilm formation [24,25]. Our studies showed that the content of TB-EPS on the CDs-modified biocarrier was increased by 18.15%, while the content of LB-EPS was decreased by 39.72%, compared with those on the unmodified biocarrier (Fig. 3b). 3D-EEM spectroscopic analysis showed that tryptophan-like proteins at the EX/EM range of 260∼285 nm/325∼375 nm (region A) and humic acid-like substances at the EX/EM range of 250∼400 nm/420∼500 nm (region B) were the main fluorescence components of EPS [26]. The fluorescence analysis found that the fluorescence intensity of its region B representing humic acid-like substances in TB-EPS and LB-EPS appeared to be obviously enhanced due to the presence of CDs-modified biocarrier (Fig. 3c–f). These results indicated that the presence of iCDs promoted TB-EPS secretion, which was beneficial for AnAOB aggregation. The humic acid-like substances have been reported to be capable of accelerating microbial extracellular electron transfer [27]. Our EIS analysis also showed that the charge transfer resistance (Rct) level of TB-EPS on CDs-modified biocarrier in R2 was lower than that on unmodified biocarrier in R1(Fig. 3g), which was attributed to the increased content of humic acid-like substances and the release of iCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transport system and activities of key enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: Further studies showed that iCDs addition resulted in 21.58% increase in the ETSA of biofilm cells. Moreover, the activities of intracellular NADH dehydrogenase (Complex I), complex bc1 (Complex III) and Cytc were increased by 205.63%, 34.95% and 163.48% on CDs-modified biocarrier, respectively, compared with those on the unmodified biocarrier (Fig. 3h). The key enzymes involved in nitrogen metabolism of anammox were also analyzed. The process is usually divided into three steps: (1) nitrite is reduced to NO catalyzed by NIR, (2) NO and ammonium are converted to hydrazine catalyzed by HZS, and (3) finally hydrazine is converted to N2 catalyzed by HDH, while nitrite is oxidized to nitrate catalyzed by NXR [28]. Our studies showed that the activities of the NIR, HZS and HDH were increased by 223.09%, 32.98% and 49.80%, respectively, after the addition of iCDs (Fig. 3i). However, there was no significant change in NXR activity. These findings indicated that iCDs could accelerate intracellular electron transfer and enhance the activities of key enzymes, ultimately promoting the anammox process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of microbial community in biofilm
Microbial community analysis at the phylum and genus levels
ce:section.ce:para: Metagenomic sequencing analysis showed that the phyla Planctomycetota, Pseudomonadota and Chloroflexota were predominant in two reactors (Fig. 4 a). After 102 days operation of the two reactors, the relative abundance of Planctomycetota in the biofilm attached on CDs-modified biocarrier was increased by 11.62%, compared with that of unmodified biocarrier. The abundance of Pseudomonadota was increased from 11.20% in seeding sludge to 23.14% in the biofilm of R1 and 23.59% in the biofilm of R2, respectively. AnAOB, affiliated to the phylum Planctomycetota, was tightly associated with nitrogen removal [29]. Pseudomonadota and AnAOB have been reported to provide beneficial substances each other by cross-feeding [30,31]. The population of Chloroflexota capable of utilizing soluble microbial products and EPS [32,33], accounted for 20.14% and 17.82% in the biofilm of R1 and R2, respectively.

At genus level, the top 25 dominant genera were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4b, the relative abundances of genera Ca. Brocadia, Ca. Jettenia, Ca. Kuenenia and Ca. Scalindua in the biofilm of CDs-modified biocarrier were increased by 21.51%, 22.27%, 28.18% and 28.40%, respectively, compared with those of unmodified biocarrier. Ca. Brocadia, Ca. Jettenia, Ca. Kuenenia and Ca. Scalindua have been reported to be the most typical anammox genera [34]. Among them, Ca. Brocadia could reproduce rapidly at high substrate concentrations and exhibit a preference for growth within biofilm [35–37], and was also a key contributor to NO3 −-N transformation into NH4 +-N via endogenous DNRA [34]. It was also found that the presence of iCDs resulted in 23.98% increase in the relative abundance of Desulfobacillus belonging to the phylum Pseudomonadota. It has been reported that the carbohydrate metabolism potential of Desulfobacillus might improve the mixotrophic lifestyle of anammox community [38]. These results demonstrated that iCDs could increase the relative abundances of AnAOB and their mutualism bacteria at the phylum and the genus levels, enhancing nitrogen removal performance of anammox process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of iCDs on electron production and transport processes
ce:section.ce:para: Anammox process was driven by intracellular energy (ATP) and electron transfer of anammox community [39]. Metagenomic analysis showed that the relative abundances of genes related to ATP synthesis (atpA to atpH) were markedly improved after the addition of iCDs, in which the relative abundances of the atpB gene and atpH gene were significantly increased by 13.47% and 7.15%, respectively (Fig. 4c). Based on metatranscriptomic data, the expressions of genes involved in ATP synthesis (atpA to atpE) in the presence of iCDs were highly upregulated (Fig. 4d). For the genes related with electron transport system (ETS) (Fig. 4c) [40], the relative abundances of cyoA, cyoB, and ccoP genes encoding Cytc in the presence of iCDs were significantly increased by 338.89%, 26.04% and 26.86%, respectively. Furthermore, iCDs addition resulted in 8.03%, 12.06%, 9.11% and 14.56% increases in the relative abundances of nuoB/E/F/J genes encoding Complex Ⅰ, respectively. Among the above four genes, significant upregulation of nuoB and nuoJ genes expression was observed with a log2FC (R2/R1) of 1.00 and 1.09, respectively. Although no apparent enrichment in the presence of iCDs was observed in the relative abundance of the petB gene encoding Complex III, the expression of petB gene was dramatically increased with log2FC of 1.95 (Fig. 4d). These results revealed that iCDs promoted the enrichment and expressions of genes related with ATP and ETS, accelerating electron transfer and metabolic rate of cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of iCDs on nitrogen metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: Genes involved in nitrogen metabolism of bacterial communities in the biofilms were analyzed based on the metagenomic and metatranscriptomic data. Fig. 5 a shows that the relative abundances of genes encoding enzymes involved anammox, denitrification, nitrification and DNRA in nitrogen metabolism. The major nitrogen pathways and the variation trend of genes involved in nitrogen conversion under the influence of iCDs were shown in Fig. 5b. Specifically, the relative abundances of the nirS gene encoding NIR, hzsA/B/C gene encoding HZS, hao gene encoding hydroxylamine oxidoreductase (HAO), hdh gene encoding HDH and nxrA gene encoding NXR in anammox process were remarkably increased by 14.96%, 23.98%, 21.44%, 24.35%, 13.32%, 22.38% and 18.94%, respectively (Fig. 5a). Meanwhile, it was also found that iCDs addition resulted in 12.35%, 17.36%, 24.02% and 24.70% increases in the relative abundances of narG/H gene, napA gene and nrfA gene related with DNRA, respectively, indicating that iCDs could remarkably enhance the DNRA pathway in anammox process (Fig. 5b). Conversely, the relative abundances of norB and norC genes as the key genes in denitrification process were decreased by 16.19% and 53.64%, respectively. The distribution of the above these functional genes at genus level was further analyzed. Fig. 5c shows that the relative abundance of hdh gene in Ca. Jettenia was increased by 17.20 %. Furthermore, the relative abundances of hzsA/B/C gene, hao gene and hdh gene in Ca. Brocadia were increased by 23.98%, 21.44%, 24.35%, 24.14% and 50.12%, respectively. Interestingly, Ca. Brocadia was dominant contributor to the abundance of nrfA gene, accounting for more than 85% in the nrfA gene of both biofilms. Moreover, in the presence of iCDs, the relative abundances of narH and nrfA genes in Ca. Brocadia was significantly increased by 21.04% and 28.71%, respectively (Fig. 5c). These results indicated that iCDs enhanced the enrichment of genes not only involved in anammox process, but also related with DNRA process for the removal of nitrate as anammox by-product.

Diverse nitrogen conversion coexisted in the system and the expressions of the related genes changed remarkably in nitrogen cycle (Fig. 5d and e). Obviously, the expressions of hzsA/B/C gene, hao gene, hdh gene and nxrA gene, related to anammox, were dramatically improved with log2FC of 1.79, 1.80, 2.01, 1.56, 2.51 and 1.78, respectively (Fig. 5d). The expressions of these functional genes related with nitrogen metabolism in different genera were further analyzed. Fig. 5f shows that the expressions of hzsA/B/C gene, hao gene, hdh gene, nxrA gene, narH gene and nrfA gene in Ca. Brocadia were remarkably upregulated 3.46-, 3.49-, 4.02-, 2.94-, 5.68-, 3.47-, 2.93- and 2.00-fold, respectively. It was also observed that the expression of hdh gene in Ca. Jettenia was significantly increased with log2FC of 2.52. On the contrary, the expressions of denitrification-related genes in denitrifying bacteria were dramatically attenuated. Especially, the expressions of narG/H, napA, nirS/K and nosZ genes in Desulfobacillus were significantly diminished with log2FC of −1.08, −1.25, −1.20, −0.77, −1.12 and −0.59, respectively. Besides, the expressions of narG/H, napA, nirS/K, norB and nosZ genes in Zeimonas were also remarkably downregulated with log2FC of −0.48, −1.24, −1.59, −0.91, −1.37, −1.25 and −0.87, respectively. These results indicated that the nitrate removal was mainly attributed to the endogenous DNRA of Ca. Brocadia rather than denitrification under no exogenous organic carbon sources (Fig. 2a, b and Fig. 5f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of iCDs on carbon metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: AnAOB, autotrophic microorganisms, could convert carbon dioxide to acetyl-CoA via Wood-Ljungdahl pathway [41,42]. Our studies showed that the relative abundances of genes related with Wood-Ljungdahl pathway exhibited remarkable improvement after the addition of iCDs (Fig. 6 a). Specifically, the relative abundances of fdh gene encoding formate dehydrogenase, acsB/E gene encoding acetyl-CoA synthase and cdh gene encoding CO dehydrogenase were significantly increased by 17.68%, 12.70%, 31.25% and 11.66%, respectively (Fig. 6a). Further investigation showed that the expressions of above corresponding genes were dramatically upregulated 1.82-, 3.33-, 3.94- and 3.95-fold, respectively (Fig. 6b and c). It has been reported that acetyl-CoA is a key intermediate for the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and glycolysis pathway in AnAOB [41]. Thus, acetyl-CoA formation was beneficial to TCA cycle and glycolysis metabolism pathway (Fig. 6b). In the TCA cycle, acnA/B, IDH1, sucA/B/C/D, sdhA/B/C, fumA, mqo and mdh genes were significantly enriched after the amendment of iCDs (Fig. 6a). Among these genes, the fumA gene encoding fumarate hydratase in the presence of iCDs showed the highest expression with log2FC of 1.20 (Fig. 6b and c). Likewise, the relative abundances of porA/B/C/D, ppdK, FBP and GPI genes related with glycolysis pathway were significantly increased by 13.63%, 17.69%, 37.19%, 14.16%, 5.50%, 6.78% and 6.21%, respectively (Fig. 6a). Glucose-6P acts as a bridge linking the glycolysis pathway and glycogen metabolism (Fig. 6b). It has been reported that gluconeogenesis is an essential pathway to synthesize glycogen in Ca. Brocadia [6], and it could utilize intracellular glycogen to convert NO3 −-N to NH4 +-N via DNRA [5]. Further analysis showed that the expressions of glgB gene encoding glycogen synthesis and malQ gene encoding glycogen metabolism in Ca. Brocadia were dramatically increased with log2FC of 1.78 and 1.46, respectively (Fig. S4a). TEM image analysis also confirmed that the amount of glycogen in AnAOB was obviously decreased after iCDs addition (Fig. S4b and c). These results implied that iCDs could accelerate carbon metabolism, and improve the production and consumption rates of the glycogen, thereby enhancing the endogenous DNRA pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of iCDs on signaling molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Quorum sensing (QS) has been reported to strengthen the anammox process [43]. Thus, diverse types of genes encoding signaling molecules synthesis were analyzed. Our results showed that the relative abundances of HdtS gene encoding AHLs synthesis, LuxS gene encoding autoinducer-2 (AI-2) synthesis, RpfF gene encoding diffusible signal factor (DSF) synthesis and DGC gene encoding bis-(3′-5′)-cyclic dimeric guanosine monophosphate (c-di-GMP) synthesis were significantly increased by 112.02%, 180.64%, 4.74% and 8.83%, respectively (Fig. 6d). Meanwhile, there was no apparent change in the relative abundance of PDE gene encoding c-di-GMP decomposition. The distribution of the relative abundances of genes related to signaling molecules at genus level was further analyzed. Fig. 6e shows that the genus Ca. Brocadia was a major contributor to the abundance of DGC gene with an elevation of 23.89%. Meanwhile, the relative abundances of DGC, PDE and RpfF genes in Desulfobacillus were obviously increased by 23.58%, 22.87% and 22.05%, respectively (Fig. 6e). Previous studies have reported that the genus Desulfobacillus belonging to the phylum Pseudomonadota could supply useful materials to AnAOB by cross-feeding [44].

The expressions of genes encoding signaling molecules at genus level were further analyzed (Fig. 6f). Among these genes, the expression of DGC gene in Ca. Brocadia were dramatically enhanced with log2FC of 1.16. Interestingly, there were remarkable upregulation of 2.80-, 2.59- and 2.64-fold in the expressions of LuxI, DGC and RpfF genes, respectively, in the genus UBA12294 (Fig. 6f). It has been reported that the genus UBA12294, affiliated to the phylum Chloroflexota, could strengthen the connection with AnAOB and facilitate biofilm formation [45]. Similarly, the elevated secretion of TB-EPS and rapid biofilm formation were observed in this study (Fig. 3b). This phenomenon might be related to the overexpression of various signaling molecules in the genus Ca. Brocadia and UBA12294 after the addition of iCDs.

Based on the above results, the proposed mechanism of the enhanced nitrogen removal was that iCDs was released and partly enter AnAOB (Ca. Brocadia) to accelerate intracellular electron transfer for CO2 fixation and glycogen metabolism, and then the produced electrons were provided for anammox and endogenous DNRA processes mediated by intracellular CDs in AnAOB (Ca. Brocadia), finally resulting in the increased nitrogen removal (Fig. 6g). Previous studies showed that biochar could accelerate anammox process via accelerating extracellular electron mediator [46]. Moreover, the biochar was also reported to be capable of improving endogenous DNRA process of AnAOB under the COD/N ratio range of 0.1∼0.5 [47]. However, in the absence of organic carbon sources, endogenous DNRA process of AnAOB is difficult to proceed rapidly. Different from biochar, CDs with the sizes of less than 10 nm could enter cells to accelerate intracellular electron transfer. The present study demonstrated that the iCDs could accelerate the endogenous DNRA process without an external organic carbon source via entering cells, thereby effectively saving energy and reduce costs. Notably, iCDs exhibited high nitrogen removal rates (NRR) under higher NLR conditions compared to N-graphene, reduced graphene oxide, biochar and Fe, Mn-biochar (Fig. S5) [7,48,49].

At bacterial community level, the iCDs impacted microbial communications via upregulating the abundances and expressions of genes related to QS, which promoted the secretion of signaling molecules, TB-EPS production and biofilm formation. Due to the release of the iCDs, the TB-EPS of the biofilm attached on CDs-modified biocarrier exhibited good electrical conductivity, facilitating the extracellular electron transfer and enhancing various bacteria communication. Especially, iCDs enhanced the enrichment of Ca. Brocadia, and facilitated QS of Ca. Brocadia and their mutualism bacteria. These studies showed that the slow-release property of CDs immobilized on biocarriers provided a good electron transfer environment, and facilitated continuous supplement of CDs for maintaining high activity levels of AnAOB. Therefore, iCDs was of potential application for nitrogen removal via anammox process.

This study initially introduced CDs-functionalized biocarrier into anammox reactor. The iCDs addition resulted in 7.53%∼58.48% increase in total nitrogen removal. During this process, the iCDs was slowly released and partly entered cells. The behavior of the iCDs not only improved TB-EPS conductivity, facilitating extracellular electron transfer, but also enhanced the activities of key enzymes related to anammox process. Meanwhile, the CDs enhanced glycogen metabolism pathways, anammox and the endogenous DNRA in the enriched Ca. Brocadia, finally enhancing nitrogen removal. These findings revealed the accelerating effect and mechanism of iCDs, and provided a new avenue for highly efficient nitrogen removal in anammox process.

Fanghao Cheng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hong Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qiansheng Li: Software. Xiaoman Li: Investigation. Jichao Wang: Formal analysis. Yifan Li: Methodology. Chaoyu Li: Methodology. Jiti Zhou: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176027). The authors thank Dr. Xiaona Li for the technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.163051.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
